picks.name: NVIDIA Corporation
picks.rationale: NVDA is the irreplaceable spine of the AI capex supercycle: reported $1.866 EPS in May 2026 (beating consensus by $0.114), has beaten Wall Street EPS estimates in 21 of the past 23 quarters, and guided Q2 revenue at $91B ±2%. The four largest hyperscalers guided collective 2026 capex of ~$725B — up 77% YoY — with most earmarked for AI infrastructure directly fueling NVDA demand. S&P Global just upgraded NVDA's credit rating citing 'insatiable demand' for AI systems. After a week of sector-wide chip selloffs, NVDA is recovering at $205 — a tactically attractive re-entry inside its 52-week range of $140.86–$236.54, with the secular thesis fully intact.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2027 earnings print (late August) with Blackwell Ultra ramp confirmation and hyperscaler order book update
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$205, off ~13% from 52-week high of $236.54; recovering from semiconductor sector rotation selloff; 62 analysts maintain Strong Buy with avg target of $298.42 implying ~45% upside; supply constraints are the ceiling, not demand
picks.exitTrigger: Any major hyperscaler (Meta, Google, Amazon, Microsoft) issues a capex guidance cut; U.S. export control expansion materially reduces China-addressable TAM; gross margin falls below 70%
picks.keyRisk: U.S. export controls on advanced compute chips to China — already partially in effect — could cut 15–20% of incremental revenue in a single policy announcement

picks.name: Alphabet Inc. (Class A)
picks.rationale: GOOGL is the single name with the broadest smart-money consensus in the Q1 2026 13F cycle: Berkshire tripled its stake (up 224% to a $16.6B position, now its 7th-largest holding), while Tepper's Appaloosa has it as a top-5 holding and Tiger Global lists it as its largest disclosed position. At $361, GOOGL trades well off its 52-week high of $408.61, offering a rare entry point into a business with Search dominance, YouTube advertising resilience, Google Cloud accelerating, and Gemini AI monetization ramping — all at a far more reasonable multiple than semiconductor peers (XLC communication services carry sub-25x fwd P/E vs. semis at 35–85x). RBC Capital recently upgraded Communication Services to Overweight.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — first full quarter with Gemini AI integration across Search and Cloud; Google Cloud growth rate expected to re-accelerate above 30% YoY
picks.entryNote: Trading at $361, ~11.7% below 52-week high of $408.61; Berkshire added 224% in Q1 at approximately this price range; P/E materially below mega-cap tech median; technically recovering from Iran-shock lows
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 25% YoY; DOJ antitrust remedy forces Search distribution breakup; AI Overviews materially cannibalize click-through ad revenue at scale
picks.keyRisk: DOJ antitrust structural remedy — a forced Search or Android divestiture would be a multi-year overhang and potential multiple compression event

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom reported Q2 FY2026 revenue of $22.19B (up 48% YoY) with AI semiconductor revenue surging 143% YoY representing 68% of total revenue, record operating margin of 67.3%, and FCF of $10.3B. Management guided Q3 consolidated revenue of $29.4B (up 84% YoY) and reiterated FY26 AI semiconductor revenue of $56B (~+180% YoY). With 35 of 42 analysts at Strong Buy, AVGO is differentiated from NVDA: it wins on custom ASIC silicon for hyperscalers (Google TPUs, Meta MTIA) — complementary, not competing exposure. The stock sold off post-earnings on expectations overreach, creating a tactical entry opportunity.
picks.catalyst: Q3 FY2026 earnings (September) confirming $29.4B revenue guide and FY27 AI ASIC design win pipeline; any hyperscaler announcement of next-gen custom chip program with Broadcom
picks.entryNote: Stock dropped sharply post-Q2 beat on elevated buy-side expectations bar — a classic washout of weak hands; trading at moderate extended flag level; EBITDA margins at 69% of revenue; 35/42 analysts Strong Buy with avg target ~$470
picks.exitTrigger: Gross margin falls below 73% on mix shift to lower-margin AI chips; hyperscaler customer consolidates ASIC design away from Broadcom to internal silicon; VMware integration generates unexpected revenue churn above 10%
picks.keyRisk: Gross margin declined 230 bps to 77.1% on AI chip mix shift — continued compression toward 73% would signal structural margin impairment and trigger multiple de-rating

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: Amazon is the consensus highest-conviction name across the smartest money in the Q1 2026 13F cycle: Tepper nearly doubled his stake (+98%, making it ~15% of Appaloosa), Ackman added 19% making it his #2 position, and Tiger Global lists it among its top 5. AWS margin expansion is the core thesis — cloud hyperscaler spending drives incremental margin, not cost, and the $725B collective hyperscaler capex guided for 2026 lands disproportionately on AWS infrastructure. Advertising is the stealth second driver, growing 18%+ YoY. At current prices, AMZN offers multi-year EPS compounding at a more digestible valuation than pure-play AI hardware.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — AWS revenue growth rate and operating income margin expansion vs. consensus; any announcement of Trainium3 availability or next-gen AI inference offering
picks.entryNote: Off highs after broad tech rotation; AWS margin expansion from ~37% to 40%+ remains the multi-year earnings driver; three of the most sophisticated long-only and macro funds added aggressively in Q1 2026 at current price levels
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 18% YoY for two consecutive quarters; Amazon advertising revenue growth falls below 12% YoY signaling consumer ad budget pullback; retail operating losses widen materially on wage inflation
picks.keyRisk: Consumer spending slowdown from sticky inflation (CPI at 4.2%) compresses North America retail margins and offsets AWS strength — a simultaneous revenue and margin headwind across both segments

picks.name: Oracle Corporation
picks.rationale: Oracle reported record Q4 and FY2026 results with Q4 Cloud Infrastructure (IaaS) revenue of $5.8B (up 93% YoY), full-year operating cash flow surging 54% to a record $32B, and RPO surging to $638B (up 363%) — one of the largest backlog builds in enterprise software history providing extraordinary revenue visibility. Management guided FY2027 total revenue growth of 34% in constant currency with Q1 cloud revenue growing 58–64%. The 10% post-earnings selloff on near-term margin compression is a tactical gift: gross margin pressure from data center ramp is transient, while the backlog guarantees multi-year revenue execution. Technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February with Oracle named as a primary driver.
picks.catalyst: Q1 FY2027 earnings (September 2026) — first proof point on the 34% FY27 revenue growth guide; any new hyperscaler or sovereign cloud partnership announcement
picks.entryNote: Stock dropped 10% post-record beat — near-term EPS guidance was light due to data center ramp costs; this is a classic 'beat-and-guide-conservatively' washout; RPO of $638B creates a multi-year earnings floor; entry here captures the backlog at a post-dip discount
picks.exitTrigger: RPO growth decelerates below 100% YoY; OCI cloud infrastructure market share plateaus below 10%; gross margin compression extends below 70% for two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: Near-term gross margin compression as Oracle ramps data center capex to fulfill the $638B RPO — if capex overshoot delays FCF recovery beyond FY2028, the multiple will not hold

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: XOM is the highest-quality expression of the energy sector thesis in a regime where energy prices rose 23.5% YoY and energy accounted for over 60% of CPI gains. XLE is up ~26–27% YTD as the #2 sector, and XOM is the integrated oil bellwether with superior FCF generation, Permian Basin production growth, Pioneer acquisition integration, and Guyana deepwater ramp providing multi-year volume uplift. In a stagflationary regime with Iran-Hormuz risk live, energy is a natural portfolio inflation hedge that pays you a 3.5%+ dividend yield while you wait. Peace deal scenario creates a temporary oil price pullback but does not break the multi-year Permian+Guyana production thesis.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Pioneer integration synergy realization, Guyana Yellowtail volumes, and Permian production update; Iran peace deal resolution (either direction is tradable)
picks.entryNote: Energy at below-median historical P/E multiples; XLE +26% YTD confirms institutional accumulation; geopolitical risk premium in oil supports near-term earnings while Permian/Guyana underwrite long-term FCF; crude near $85/bbl post-peace-deal-hope selloff provides a reasonable entry
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude falls sustainably below $65/bbl on demand destruction or coordinated OPEC+ supply increase; Pioneer integration generates unexpected reserve write-downs; U.S. windfall profits tax legislation advances in Congress
picks.keyRisk: Iran peace deal confirmation plus OPEC+ supply restoration simultaneously — a coordinated supply surge could push Brent toward $65–70, compressing XOM FCF and dividend growth capacity

picks.name: RTX Corporation (Raytheon Technologies)
picks.rationale: RTX is the premier expression of the defense & aerospace structural tailwind: NATO allies increasing defense budgets to 2%+ of GDP, Middle East conflict driving Patriot/LTAMDS demand, and F-135 engine upgrade cycle providing multi-year revenue visibility. Industrials are up ~12% YTD as part of the real-economy rotation, and RTX's backlog exceeds $220B — a multi-year revenue floor. Unlike pure defense primes, RTX's Pratt & Whitney commercial engine segment also benefits from the global aviation recovery and aftermarket pricing power, creating a dual-engine earnings compounding story not available in LMT or NOC.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — Patriot missile contract delivery update, Pratt & Whitney GTF engine production ramp, and FY2026 guidance confirmation amid elevated geopolitical demand
picks.entryNote: Defense sector at moderate premium to 10-year median but justified by structural budget elevation; backlog at $220B+ provides exceptional revenue visibility; technically constructive within prior pattern; defense is acyclical overlay that improves the portfolio's late-cycle resilience
picks.exitTrigger: Pratt & Whitney GTF engine powder metal disk defect remediation generates unexpected liability above $3B; major NATO defense budget reversal; Iran peace deal triggers broad geopolitical risk-off for defense spending expectations
picks.keyRisk: Pratt & Whitney GTF engine powder metal quality defect — ongoing remediation could generate additional charges, production slowdowns, or customer credits that compress margins unexpectedly

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman delivered exceptional Q1 2026 results driven by a surge in trading revenues, M&A advisory rebound, and strength in asset and wealth management — with ROTCE trending back toward 15%+ and EPS beats in 4 consecutive quarters. In the current stagflationary, elevated-volatility regime, GS is structurally advantaged: elevated VIX (~19) and volatile credit markets drive FICC and equity trading revenues, while the M&A pipeline recovery benefits IBD. XLF is ~-5% YTD — deeply lagging — creating a value setup: Financials trade at a forward P/E of 16.5x, below 10-year median. The bear-steepener yield curve (2s10s +20–25 bps) is a net interest income tailwind for bank and capital markets earnings.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) — trading revenue and IB fee pipeline update; any signal of M&A dealflow acceleration from deregulation tailwinds under current administration
picks.entryNote: XLF ~-5% YTD at historically cheap valuations (16.5x fwd P/E); GS historically outperforms in high-volatility, high-rate regimes; bear steepener yield curve is a structural earnings tailwind; 4 consecutive EPS beats underpin estimate revision momentum
picks.exitTrigger: Credit spreads (HY OAS) blow through 450 bps signaling systemic credit stress; loan loss provisions surge more than 3x from current levels; trading revenues decline >20% QoQ on two consecutive quarters
picks.keyRisk: Rapid HY credit spread widening (from ~330–350 bps toward 450+ bps) would signal credit cycle turn and compress GS's leveraged finance and credit trading revenues simultaneously

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is one of the highest-quality compounders in the portfolio universe: its Ratings segment benefits directly from the elevated-rate / wide-spread environment through bond issuance volume (companies refinance at higher rates, driving ratings revenue); Market Intelligence is expanding with AI-powered data products including a new Cohere partnership announced June 8 for agentic financial workflows; and the pending Mobility segment separation (Board approved May 21) will unlock hidden sum-of-the-parts value. With 24 analysts at Strong Buy and a consensus price target of $533.76 (+24.6% from current $424), SPGI is a high-quality compounder trading ~26.7% below its 52-week high — a genuine value inside a quality-growth wrapper.
picks.catalyst: Mobility business separation completion (expected H2 2026) — catalyst for sum-of-the-parts re-rating; Q2 2026 earnings demonstrating bond issuance volume acceleration and Market Intelligence AI growth
picks.entryNote: Trading at $424, ~26.7% below 52-week high and below the 200-day MA of $475; 24 analysts Strong Buy; separation of Mobility segment is an identifiable catalyst for NAV unlock; bond issuance tailwind from elevated rate environment supports Ratings revenue
picks.exitTrigger: Bond issuance volume collapses (credit freeze scenario); DOJ raises fresh antitrust concerns about Ratings duopoly; Mobility separation generates unexpected tax liability or value destruction
picks.keyRisk: A hard landing scenario would crater bond issuance volume — the Ratings segment's primary revenue driver — while also compressing Market Intelligence enterprise software budgets simultaneously

picks.name: Merck & Co. Inc.
picks.rationale: MRK at $120.33 trades near its 52-week high of $125.14 yet has significantly underperformed relative to the S&P — the healthcare sector is ~-3% YTD vs. a strong SPY — with a 2.7% dividend yield providing income while the thesis develops. Keytruda (pembrolizumab) remains the world's best-selling oncology drug with pipeline extensions across 20+ indications; the Daiichi-Sankyo ADC partnership (fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan) adds a multi-billion pipeline leg. The healthcare sector is showing improving earnings momentum with analysts raising expectations across devices, services, and pharma. MRK specifically faces Keytruda IRA pricing pressure starting 2028, but the 2–4 year horizon captures the pipeline ramp before that headwind materializes.
picks.catalyst: Pembrolizumab Phase 3 readout in earlier-stage cancers (adjuvant settings) expanding label; any positive data from the MK-1084 KRAS inhibitor program; FDA approval of subcutaneous Keytruda formulation
picks.entryNote: P/E of 33.99x with 2.7% yield; trading near 52-week high of $125.14 but meaningfully underperformed broader market YTD; healthcare sector at ~19.94x fwd P/E vs. 10-year median of ~22x — sector-level cheap; Gilead/MRK Trodelvy trial discontinuation removes an overhang; entry here is defensively positioned within healthcare
picks.exitTrigger: Keytruda patent cliff accelerates beyond current IRA pricing expectations; major Phase 3 oncology trial failure across two or more lead pipeline assets; FDA clinical hold on any Merck product
picks.keyRisk: IRA 'maximum fair price' negotiation for Keytruda beginning 2026–2028 could set a precedent far more aggressive than current $274/month estimate, materially compressing Merck's largest revenue stream earlier than modeled

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the cleanest macro hedge available in the current stagflationary regime: CPI at 4.2% and rising, the real policy rate at +125–150 bps above neutral, fiscal dominance concerns with U.S. debt/GDP above 120%, Middle East tensions, and EM central bank de-dollarization all sustain structural demand. GLD carries zero counterparty risk, no earnings risk, and no multiple risk. In genuine risk-off episodes — including the mid-March VIX spike above 30 — gold consistently outperforms equities. Iran escalation and Fed hike risk are both independently bullish for gold.
picks.catalyst: July 14 CPI print (June data) — if headline CPI prints above 4.5%, gold re-rates as the stagflation hedge of choice; any deterioration in Iran peace deal talks reigniting geopolitical premium
picks.catalystWindow: July 14, 2026 CPI print; ongoing geopolitical newsflow
picks.entryNote: Gold benefits from the bear-steepener environment (rising term premium = rising real debt concern), DXY near 99.77 with downside risk from twin deficits, and VIX at 19.25 suggesting residual investor nervousness that could spike; zero correlation to equity earnings risk
picks.exitTrigger: Iran peace deal confirmed AND Fed pivots to explicit easing bias AND core CPI prints below 2.5% for two consecutive months — a triple macro relief valve that removes all three gold tailwinds simultaneously
picks.keyRisk: A rapid and credible Iran peace deal resolution causes crude to fall sharply, headline CPI collapses ~150 bps, and Fed pivot narrative returns — temporarily removing the stagflation gold premium

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides a mechanically screened quality-income sleeve: screens for dividend consistency, free cash flow coverage, and return on equity — filtering out leveraged zombie companies. At ~3.8% dividend yield, it provides carry while the growth book compounds. Beta of ~0.65 vs. S&P provides material downside cushioning in a late-cycle selloff. Approximately 30% exposure to Industrials and Consumer Staples with low tech concentration means SCHD diversifies the portfolio away from the AI-heavy growth book. Income-focused investors face a real dilemma as bond yields surge, making SCHD's dividend growth story a natural complement.
picks.catalyst: Any Fed dovish pivot or peace deal — rate-sensitive dividend stocks re-rate; dividend growth compounding is a time-independent catalyst with each quarterly reinvestment
picks.entryNote: ~3.8% yield provides meaningful carry vs. zero ERP environment; beta 0.65 makes it the portfolio's primary equity cushion; low tech concentration (unlike SPY at ~40% tech) means it won't fall with the growth book in a semis rotation; cheaply valued vs. growth indices
picks.exitTrigger: Dividend cuts spread across constituent Industrials and Staples from a severe recession; sustained 10-year yield above 5.5% makes the 3.8% yield uncompetitive even with growth optionality
picks.keyRisk: A stagflation scenario where both dividend yields and equity prices fall simultaneously — if the Fed raises rates materially, even quality dividend payers face multiple compression alongside bond competition

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (Class B)
picks.rationale: BRK.B is the portfolio's fortress-balance-sheet anchor: $397.4B in cash and T-bills as of March 31 (earning ~5% risk-free), providing a massive earnings floor independent of equity market volatility. The diversified earnings base — GEICO insurance (hard market tailwind), BNSF railroad, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, and a $263B equity portfolio — is genuinely all-weather. Under new CEO Greg Abel, BRK.B has shown strategic dynamism: tripling into Alphabet, initiating a $2.65B Delta position, and restarting share buybacks ($234M in Q1). Trades at ~1.5x book value with ~21x P/E — undemanding for the quality. In risk-off episodes, BRK.B historically becomes a flight-to-quality destination.
picks.catalyst: Any risk-off equity selloff — BRK.B becomes the institutional flight-to-quality vehicle; Q2 2026 earnings (August) confirming deployment of cash hoard into equities or acquisitions; share buyback continuation
picks.entryNote: Trades at ~1.5x book — historically moderate premium; $397.4B cash war chest creates an earnings floor of ~$20B/year in risk-free interest; insurance hard market (pricing power) inflating GEICO earnings; Abel's first full year of leadership creates optionality for major capital deployment
picks.exitTrigger: Cash hoard deployed into a poorly valued mega-acquisition at peak cycle; GEICO reserve deficiency emerges from auto insurance underwriting; BNSF volumes collapse on deep recession
picks.keyRisk: Abel deploys the $397.4B cash war chest into a major acquisition at cyclically elevated prices — destroying the core value-creation discipline that is central to the BRK.B investment thesis

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF
picks.rationale: With the 10-year Treasury at 4.53%, IEF offers genuine income (current yield ~4.4%) with a duration of ~7.5 years — materially less vulnerable than TLT (18+ years duration) to fiscal dominance tail events or a Fed hike surprise. In the scenario where the Iran peace deal materializes and headline CPI collapses ~150 bps, the Fed's hike narrative reverses rapidly and IEF appreciates significantly. This creates an asymmetric defensive position: if the growth book underperforms in a risk-off tape, IEF provides both income and capital appreciation. The belly of the curve (7–10yr) offers better risk-reward than the 20yr+ end where fiscal dominance and Japanese bond competition are structural headwinds.
picks.catalyst: Iran peace deal confirmation causing oil to fall sharply → CPI deceleration → Fed hike narrative reversal → Treasury rally; or July 14 CPI print showing core deceleration below 2.5%
picks.catalystWindow: July 14, 2026 CPI print; Iran peace deal resolution
picks.entryNote: 10-year at 4.53% — highest real income from Treasuries in 15 years; IEF's 7.5yr duration provides meaningful price appreciation in a rate rally without the tail-risk vulnerability of TLT at 18yr duration; modest negative correlation to equity risk-off amplifies its defensive value
picks.exitTrigger: Core CPI accelerates above 3.5% for two consecutive months forcing Fed to formally signal a rate hike cycle; 10-year yield breaks sustainably above 5.25%; U.S. sovereign credit rating downgrade
picks.keyRisk: Fed formally pivots to a hiking bias at the July 28-29 FOMC meeting — with the June dot plot already revised hawkishly upward and futures pricing a December hike — IEF would face both duration loss and income reinvestment headwind simultaneously

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the portfolio's consumer staples anchor: it has demonstrated pricing power through three consecutive years of inflation with gross margins holding above 58%, sells in 200+ countries providing natural USD weakness hedging (a growing risk given the DXY downtrend bias from twin deficits), and yields ~3.1% with 62 consecutive years of dividend growth. As the single largest equity holding for Warren Buffett across all his decades, KO has a proven role as a late-cycle defensive compounder. In a regime where lower-income Americans are financially squeezed by 4.2% CPI, beverage consumption patterns typically hold — people trade down to in-home beverages, a KO stronghold.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) — organic revenue growth and pricing power confirmation; any USD weakening providing international revenue translation tailwind; trade-down consumer behavior benefiting packaged beverages
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~22x forward earnings — premium to historical but justified by 62-year dividend growth streak and pricing power demonstrated across the inflation cycle; ~3.1% yield provides carry; low beta (~0.55) amplifies its portfolio cushion role
picks.exitTrigger: Organic volume growth turns negative for two consecutive quarters signaling demand destruction even in staples; GLP-1 drug adoption meaningfully reduces caloric beverage consumption at scale; gross margin falls below 55% from input cost surge
picks.keyRisk: GLP-1 drug (Ozempic/Wegovy) mass adoption reducing caloric beverage demand at scale — a 5–10 year secular risk that could structurally impair the sugary beverage volume growth assumption

summary: The portfolio is positioned for a late-cycle stagflationary regime where headline CPI is re-accelerating to 4.2%, the Fed has abandoned its easing bias (futures now pricing a December hike), real rates are restrictive at +125–150 bps above neutral, and geopolitical energy shocks from the Iran conflict are embedding into PCE. The 10-position growth book expresses high conviction in the AI capex supercycle (NVDA, AVGO, ORCL), the AI software monetization layer at reasonable relative valuations (GOOGL, AMZN), real-economy inflation beneficiaries (XOM, RTX), financial complexity plays (GS, SPGI), and a defensive healthcare value position (MRK) — together spanning 5 distinct GICS sectors with complementary end-market exposures. All seven prior high-conviction names (NVDA, GOOGL, AVBO, AMZN, ORCL, XOM, RTX, GS) are carried over with theses intact; SPGI is retained at medium conviction given its Mobility separation catalyst, and MRK stays as the healthcare value anchor; META was dropped from the top 10 growth book this session to accommodate better sector diversification (3-name IT cap rule respected across NVDA+AVGO+ORCL). The dominant risk being underwritten is the simultaneous scenario where Iran peace deal collapses AND core CPI accelerates above 3.2% on the July 14 print, forcing the Fed to formally signal a hike at the July 28-29 FOMC — a triple headwind that would compress growth multiples, widen HY spreads above 400 bps, and reprice the equity risk premium sharply negative.
diversificationNote: The 10-name growth book spans Information Technology (NVDA, AVGO, ORCL — at the 3-name sector cap), Communication Services (GOOGL), Consumer Discretionary (AMZN), Energy (XOM), Industrials (RTX), Financials (GS, SPGI — two names but in distinct sub-sectors: capital markets vs. financial data infrastructure), and Health Care (MRK) — 7 distinct GICS sectors, well above the 5-sector minimum; within IT, NVDA/AVGO/ORCL are intentionally complementary (GPU silicon, custom ASIC silicon, cloud infrastructure software) rather than competing for the same dollar; the defensive sleeve spans gold commodity (GLD), dividend quality equity (SCHD), fortress conglomerate (BRK.B), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples (KO) — covering all 5 required defensive exposure categories with zero duplication.

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro briefing for **Friday, June 13, 2026**:

---

## 📊 MACRO DATA DASHBOARD — U.S. EQUITIES REGIME BRIEF

---

### 🔵 FED POLICY
- **Current Fed Funds Rate:** 4.25–4.50% (held since late 2025; the March 2026 dot plot projected 
the median fed funds rate falling only 25 bps in 2026 and 25 bps in 2027
).
- **June 2026 Dot Plot (latest SEP):** 
In 2026, rates are now projected at 3.6% (revised up from 3.4%), and 3.4% in 2027
, reflecting a markedly more hawkish path than even March. 
PCE inflation is estimated to hit 3.0% by year-end 2025, with 2026 easing to only 2.4%.

- **Market-Implied Path (Fed Funds Futures):** 
Per CME FedWatch, futures traders now expect no rate cuts at all in 2026; earlier this year, odds favored at least one quarter-point cut, and the FOMC may even consider rate hikes.
 
Following the May CPI report, futures markets priced in the likelihood that the next move will be a *hike* in December.

- **Real Policy Rate:** With core PCE at ~3.0% and the policy rate at 4.25–4.50%, the real rate is approximately **+125–150 bps** — meaningfully above most estimates of neutral (~0.5–1.0% real), i.e., policy is **genuinely restrictive**.

---

### 🔵 YIELD CURVE
- **10-Year Treasury:** 
The yield on the 10-year Treasury note edged down to **4.53%** on Thursday (June 11).

- **30-Year Treasury:** Based on the most recent available data, 
the 30-year was at approximately **5.01%**
 in early April and has tracked higher since.
- **2-Year Treasury:** Approximately **4.25–4.35%** (tracking closely to the policy rate).
- **2s10s Spread:** Modestly positive (~+20–25 bps), having just re-inverted and re-steepened in recent weeks — a "bear steepener" driven by long-end term premium expansion, not by easing expectations.
- **3m10y:** Still near zero or slightly negative — the recession-warning spread has not fully normalized.
- **Term Premium Direction:** Rising — 
US PPI rose 6.5% YoY in May (highest since Nov 2022), with markets reinforcing expectations the Fed could raise rates this year.


---

### 🔴 INFLATION — RE-ACCELERATING
- **Headline CPI (May '26):** 
The annual inflation rate rose to **4.2%** in May 2026, its highest since April 2023, up from 3.8% in April — the *third consecutive monthly acceleration* — driven by energy costs jumping 23.5% due to the Iran conflict.

- **Core CPI (May '26):** 
Core CPI climbed to **2.9% YoY**, matching forecasts and reaching its highest since September 2025.
 
Month-over-month, core rose 0.2% in May after a 0.4% surge in April.

- **PCE (Feb '26, FOMC staff):** 
Total PCE inflation was 2.8% and core PCE was 3.0% in February.

- **Supercore (Services ex-shelter):** 
Services less energy services rose 0.3% MoM after 0.5% in April
 — some deceleration but still sticky. 
Shelter rose only 0.3% MoM, half of April's gain.

- **Trajectory: RE-ACCELERATING** — energy-driven at the headline; core is grinding higher on services and shelter. 
The Fed discounts energy in its deliberations, but will note that core inflation is "likely to creep upwards as the year progresses."


---

### 🔵 GROWTH — RESILIENT BUT DECELERATING
- **Atlanta Fed GDPNow (Q2 2026):** 
The GDPNow model estimated Q2 2026 real GDP growth at **4.3% SAAR** as of May 21
, down from higher early readings but still robust. 
Q1 2026 BEA advance estimate came in at 2.0% SAAR.

- **Payrolls:** 
Unemployment held at 4.4% through February; average monthly payroll change was low in Jan–Feb due to a healthcare sector strike and harsh weather, with effects expected to unwind.
 
Average hourly earnings rose 3.8% YoY through February.

- **ISM Manufacturing/Services:** Incorporated into the GDPNow update of May 21; manufacturing has been in borderline contraction/expansion territory, while services remain the primary growth engine.

---

### 🟡 CREDIT & LIQUIDITY — MIXED
- **HY OAS:** Spreads spiked sharply in March on Iran war fears (briefly above 400 bps) and have partially retraced but remain elevated at ~330–350 bps — well above the 250–280 bps tights seen in late 2025. Direction: **widening bias** on rate hike fears + geopolitical risk.
- **IG OAS:** ~115–125 bps, modestly wider vs. Q4 2025 tights.
- 
Per Morgan Stanley, "credit spreads have continued their widening trend since late January as concerns about AI disruption and private credit" weighed.

- **DXY:** 
The U.S. Dollar Index was at approximately **99.77** as of Friday morning (June 12), up from post-Iran lows.
 
The greenback drew support from expectations that rising energy prices and higher inflation reduce the likelihood of near-term rate cuts.

- **Financial Conditions:** Tightening — VIX elevated; 
the VIX was at 19.25 on June 12, with the prior close at 19.44.
 
After cresting above 30 in mid-March, VIX trended lower through April–May, but a sharp rally in early June pushed it back into the low 20s as semiconductor profit-taking and persistent inflation weighed on risk sentiment.


---

### 🟡 EARNINGS BACKDROP — SUPPORTIVE BUT DE-RATED
- **Forward S&P 500 EPS (2026):** 
Per Yardeni Research, consensus is ~$323/share for 2026 operating earnings — a **19% YoY boost** over 2025's $271.
 Wells Fargo estimates $315–$365 for 2026–2027.
- **Forward P/E:** 
The S&P 500 P/E peaked at 23x in October 2025 (95th percentile vs. 30-year history) and has since fallen to ~20x (73rd percentile).
 Trailing earnings yield (inverse of trailing P/E) is approximately 
3.14%
 — still thin versus a 4.53% 10-year yield, meaning the equity risk premium (ERP) is near zero or negative.
- **Tech Concentration Risk:** 
Ten S&P 500 stocks represent nearly 40% of the index's value — all with AI exposure — and "a majority of earnings growth for 2026 is narrowly focused in the tech sector, accounting for nearly half" of total index EPS growth.


---

### 🔴 GEOPOLITICAL / EVENT TAPE
- **Iran War / Hormuz Strait:** 
The conflict has transformed from a kinetic event to an economic siege, with the U.S. bottling up Iran's oil exports while Iran's military remnants attempt to disrupt regional shipments through Hormuz.
 
Energy prices rose 3.9% in May amid Iran war disruptions; the energy index accounted for over 60% of the overall CPI increase.

- **Peace Deal Hopes (June 12):** 
Stocks rose Friday on reports a peace deal might be near that would lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz, though markets wavered amid conflicting reports; crude oil fell ~2% to near $85/bbl.

- **Fed Hike Risk:** 
President Trump has vowed additional strikes on Iran and threatened to target Kharg Island's energy infrastructure
, a further upside inflation risk that could force the Fed's hand.

---

## ⚡ THREE-PARAGRAPH BRIEF

### 1. 🟠 REGIME CALL: **Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Transition**
The U.S. macro regime is firmly **late-cycle with stagflationary characteristics** — the rare and dangerous combination of re-accelerating inflation (headline CPI at 4.2%, core PCE at 3.0%, and trending higher) running concurrently with a Fed that has fully abandoned its 2026 easing bias: 
following the May CPI report, futures markets priced the next Fed move as a *hike* in December.
 Real rates are restrictive (~+125 bps above neutral), 
Q1 GDP came in at only 2.0% SAAR
 despite the GDPNow Q2 read of 4.3% (likely front-loaded demand), the yield curve is bear-steepening, the ERP is near zero, and geopolitical energy shocks are embedding into PCE. This is a regime where duration is dangerous, equities are expensive on a risk-adjusted basis, and the margin for error is minimal.

---

### 2. ✅ WHAT'S WORKING / ❌ WHAT'S BREAKING

**Working:** (1) **Energy / Integrated Oils** — 
energy prices up 23.5% YoY with gasoline +40.5%
 directly benefits E&P and oilfield services; (2) **Quality/AI-exposed Megacap Tech** — earnings revisions are powerfully positive; 
technology EPS estimates have been revised sharply higher since mid-February, driven by names like Oracle and Micron.
 (3) **Defense & Aerospace** — geopolitical escalation is a structural tailwind; (4) **Short-duration / floating rate credit** — with hike risk re-priced, bank loans and CLOs hold up better than HY bonds.

**Breaking:** (1) **Rate-sensitive sectors** (Utilities, REITs, Homebuilders) — negative ERP and rising long-end yields are structurally toxic; (2) **Consumer Discretionary / Lower-income consumer** — 
high inflation is squeezing households financially, with lower-income Americans particularly impacted as they spend more of stretched paychecks on necessities.
 (3) **Broad HY credit** — spreads on a widening trajectory with rate hike risk embedded; (4) **Non-AI Tech / Speculative Growth** — 
the Nasdaq 100's forward P/E has dropped 29% from its peak
 and multiple expansion is off the table entirely.

---

### 3. 👁️ WHAT TO WATCH — TOP 2–3 CATALYSTS (Next 1–6 Months)

1. **Iran Peace Deal / Hormuz Reopening (1–4 weeks):** 
News of a potential peace deal emerged Friday, with reports it could lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz.
 A confirmed deal would be the single largest near-term macro relief valve: oil falls sharply, headline CPI collapses ~150 bps within 2 months, and the Fed hike narrative reverses. Watch Polymarket odds and State Dept. communications. Failure to resolve = stagflation locked in.

2. **June FOMC Decision + Updated SEP (July 28–29 FOMC):** With futures now pricing a *hike* by December and the June dot plot already hawkishly revised, 
the FOMC may even consider rate hikes this year.
 Any signal of a formal pivot to hike bias — particularly from new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh — would immediately reprice equities (ERP collapse) and trigger a vicious bear steepener in rates. 
Warsh has indicated he thinks rates can go lower on AI productivity disinflation,
 but the data trajectory is not cooperating yet.

3. **July 14 CPI Print (June Data):** 
The June 2026 CPI report is scheduled for July 14, 2026.
 This will be the first clean read on whether the May energy shock is fading (peace deal scenario) or whether core inflation is making a new leg higher into services. A print above 4.5% headline or 3.2%+ core forces the Fed's hand and would be the most significant negative risk event of the quarter for equities.

---
*All data sourced from BLS, FOMC, Atlanta Fed, Fox Business, CNBC, TradingEconomics, WisdomTree, and Morgan Stanley as of June 12, 2026. Forward-looking statements are analytical assessments, not investment advice.*
